title: The Barn, Palm Grove, St. Helier: Planning Application (P/2025/0277): Appeal Decision
text:
A decision made 14 April 2026:Decision Reference: MD-ENV-2026-412Public Subject: Planning appeal decision: P/2025/0277 (The Barn, Palm Grove, St Helier) Report Title: Planning appeal decision: P/2025/0277 (The Barn, Palm Grove, St Helier)Public Decision(s): The Minister decided to dismiss the appeal, and to refuse planning permission (P/2025/0277), subject to the refusal reasons being reduced and revised, in accordance with the inspector’s recommendations.Reason for Decision(s):Having considered all aspects of the appeal the Minister decided to give effect to the inspector’s recommendations and was satisfied that there were no reasons not to do so.Resource Implications: There are no new financial and/or staffing implications arising as a consequence of this Ministerial Decision. Action Required: The Ministerial Office to advise the Judicial Greffe of the decision.Signature: Signed By: Minister for the EnvironmentDate Signed: Date of Decision (If different from Date Signed): 

 <h1

--- Inspector's Report ---

PLANNING AND BUILDING (JERSEY) LAW 2002 (as amended)
Appeal under Article 108 against a decision to refuse planning
permission
REPORT TO THE MINISTER FOR PLANNING AND ENVIRONMENT
By Mr Philip Staddon BSc, Dip, MBA, MRTPI
___________________________________________________________
Appellant: Conor Quinn
Site address: The Barn, Palm Grove, St. Helier
Application reference number: P/2025/0277
Proposal: ‘Install 2 No. first floor windows to North Elevation. Replace 1 No First
Floor Window with Door and 'Juliet' Balcony to West Elevation and Construct 1
No. Dormer Window to East Elevation.’
Decision Notice date: 28 May 2025
Procedure: Written representations
Inspector’s site visit: 9 February 2026
Inspector’s report date: 7 April 2026
___________________________________________________________
Introduction
1. This report contains my assessment of the appeal made by Conor Quinn
against the planning authority’s decision to refuse planning permission for a
scheme of alterations and a dormer roof extension at a residential property
dwelling known as The Barn, situated at Palm Grove in St Helier.
Procedural matters
2. The appellant’s appeal form included a request for a Hearing, as he was
unable to attend the Planning Committee. However, I did not consider that
the Hearing procedure was necessary in this case and, following
consultation with the parties, I have followed the written representations
procedure.
The appeal property, the proposal, and the application
determination
The appeal property
3. The Barn is a residential property situated on the east side of Palm Grove,
which is a cul-de-sac that slopes down southwards from Manor Park Road
giving access to a small cluster of dwellings. This group of dwellings
1

includes the listed former farmhouse Palm Grove1, immediately to the west
of the appeal property. When I visited, the listed building had scaffolding
and net screening affixed to it, and appeared to be in need of repair and
refurbishment; I understand it has been in this condition for some time.
4. The appeal property is described in the appellant’s documentation2 as a ‘2-
bedroom maisonette flat’. It occupies the first and second floors within the
northern half of a converted building, which appears to have agricultural
origins; it has rendered walls and a pitched slated roof with a hip at the
northern end. The first-floor includes an open plan living area, along with a
bathroom and a separate WC. The second-floor accommodation, which is
within the roofspace, houses 2 bedrooms which are lit by 4 rooflights, 2 in
the west roof plane and 2 in the east plane. The property also includes a
detached outbuilding used for storage purposes and a modest sized
enclosed garden with a pergola structure, to its west and north-west
respectively.
5. The building also includes a residential unit on the ground floor (beneath
The Barn), and what appears to be a separate dwelling (No. 5) in the
southern part of the building, with a nameplate reading ‘The Dower House’;
that property has a modest traditional pitched roof dormer in its east facing
roof plane. There are other dwellings to the east, south, and west, and to
the north is Blanc Pignon, a single storey dwelling set at a higher level, such
that southward views from it are over the appeal property.
6. The appeal site is within the Built-Up Area as defined in the Bridging Island
Plan (BIP) (adopted March 2022).
The P/2025/0277 proposal
7. The appeal proposal seeks planning permission for a scheme of works that
comprises 3 distinct elements:
i) Extend the roof by the addition of a box dormer within the eastern
roof plane to create a bathroom.
ii) Create 2 new window openings at first floor level on the north end
elevation.
iii) Create a Juliet balcony window by extending an existing first floor
window opening on the west elevation.
The decision and review
8. Officers initially assessed the proposal to be unacceptable for 3 reasons:
Reason 1: The proposed dormer window, due to its scale, massing, and
visual dominance, would have a detrimental impact on the character of the
host building and context of the surrounding area, contrary to Policy GD6 of
the Bridging Island Plan (2022).
1 HER reference HE1169
2 Planning Statement dated 23 March 2025 produced by Planning.je Property Planning Services
2

Reason 2: The new window voids and dormer would have an unreasonable
impact on neighbouring amenity in terms of increased overlooking and loss
of privacy which conflicts with Policy GD1 of the Bridging Island Plan (2022).
Reason 3: Due to the lack of information, the potential harmful impact of
the proposal on the setting of the adjoining heritage asset cannot be fully
assessed, as required in terms of Policies SP4 and HE1 of the Bridging
Island Plan (2022).
9. The appellant then sought a Review of the decision, which was considered
by the Planning Committee at its 25 September 2025 meeting. The
Committee endorsed the officer’s decision and the 3 refusal reasons.
Mr Quinn’s appeal is made against that decision.
Summary of the appellant’s grounds of appeal
10. The appellant’s case is set out in the appeal form, a Statement with
appendices including a number of letters of support, and a document
responding to the planning authority’s case, with appendices including a
series of photographs of dormer extensions.
11. The Statement provides responses to each of the 3 reasons for refusal.
12. With regard to the first reason, the appellant disputes that the proposal is
not in keeping with the traditional nature of the surrounding area and the
host building. He states that this assessment does not accurately reflect the
existing condition of the property, as the building was already modernised
extensively over recent years before he purchased it, beyond what would
reasonably be described as traditional, meaning its established character is
already contemporary in form and appearance. He states that the proposal
was therefore developed to align with the building as it exists today, rather
than an earlier traditional form that no longer defines its character. He
submits that the proposal is therefore consistent with the BIP policies GD6
(Design Quality), SP4 (Protecting the Character of the Island), and HE1
(Protecting Listed Buildings and Places of Heritage Value).
13. Concerning the second reason, he disputes the allegation that the proposal
would increase overlooking, as all proposed windows would be fully frosted
and therefore would have no impact on the privacy of neighbouring
properties. He points out that the existing bedrooms contain four Velux
windows that directly overlook Palm Grove and the surrounding area, and
submits that the addition of the new frosted windows would not increase
overlooking in any way. The appellant includes statements from neighbours
confirming their support and agreeing that privacy would not be affected.
14. Regarding the third reason, the appellant states that it is not clear what
further information is being requested concerning the listed building.
However, in accordance with the relevant policy terms (SP4 and HE1), he
submits that the proposal is committed to preserving the special interest of
the listed building. He further notes that the listed building has remained
essentially untouched, with roof tiles missing and scaffolding in place for
over five years, which is significantly more detrimental to its condition and
surroundings than the modest works proposed for The Barn.
3

15. The Statement concludes that the scheme is compliant with the design,
character, and heritage protection requirements set out in policies GD6, SP4
and HE1 of the BIP, particularly when considered in the context of the
already extensively modernised condition of the building.
Summary of the planning authority’s case
16. The planning authority’s case is set out in a Response document with
appendices, which include the officer report and the minutes from the
September 2025 Planning Committee meeting. The Response explains that
the proposal was considered against the relevant BIP policies and initially
assessed by officers to be unacceptable for 3 reasons, which were endorsed
by the Planning Committee following the Review request process.
17. The officer report includes an analysis of each of the 3 elements of the
proposal. It assesses that the Juliet window/balcony would not give rise to
any overlooking. However, it sets out concerns about privacy impacts
arising from the proposed windows in the north elevation and the roof
dormer which would overlook adjacent properties. It explains that it
considers the dormer extension to be ‘visually incongruous’ and ‘neither
sympathetic nor subservient to the style of the host building’. It also states
that no information has been provided to assess the potential impact on the
adjacent listed building.
18. The Response does not repeat this reasoning and defers to the officer
report. However, it does respond to comments concerning the review
process and communications, and sets out its view that the planning
authority did communicate with the appellant and that the Review process
was carried out in a proper and timely manner.
19. The planning authority maintains that permission should be refused and the
appeal dismissed.
Interested parties’ views
20. I have considered and taken into account the representations made by
interested parties at the application and appeal stages. These include an
objection from the owner of Blanc Pignon to the 2 windows proposed in the
north elevation, and a number of representations from near neighbours
confirming their support for the scheme of home improvements.
Inspector’s assessment
21. As the 3 elements of the scheme raise different planning issues, I will set
out my analysis for each component, and cross reference this with the
planning authority’s respective reasons for refusal.
The dormer extension
22. The dormer extension would be positioned on the eastern roof plane, set
between the 2 skylights, and moreorless centred on the mid-point line of
the door to the ground floor flat. The officer report states that it would be
3.2m in width and 3.0m in depth, with a flat roof height of around 2.7m.
4

These dimensions appear to be a product of seeking to accommodate a full
suite in the proposed bathroom, without compromising existing bedroom
space.
23. The result is that the dormer extension is large and would occupy much of
the roof plane between the two skylights. Whilst the submitted plans and
images show that the addition would have vertical slate tiles and matching
windows, there is no escaping the fact that the dormer would appear as a
bulky, overlarge and discordant feature. I note the appellant’s submissions
that the building has been much altered and modernised. However, the
building retains its traditional proportions, some detailing, and character.
The addition would jar with these traditional proportions and character, and
would appear awkward and out of place.
24. Jersey does not currently have an adopted design guide for domestic
extensions, but such guides are commonplace in England and many deal
with roof additions and dormer extensions. The principles of successful
dormer roof extensions are largely matters of consensus, and tend to focus
on subservience to the host property, keeping the scale and width
proportionately modest, and adopting a sympathetic design, including
careful alignment with windows below to maintain visual cohesion.
25. In this case, I assess that the design departs from these good design
principles and would appear dominant, overlarge and insensitive to the host
property and its surroundings, which include a listed building. The result is
an unacceptable design which conflicts with BIP policy GD6 (Design Quality)
which, amongst other matters, requires new developments to successfully
address its relationship to existing buildings, settlement form and distinctive
characteristics of a place, having regard to the layout, form and scale.
26. With regard to the consequential impact on the adjacent listed building, I
find the planning authority’s third reason to be somewhat muddled. I have
some sympathy with the appellant’s claim that he is unclear what further
information is required.
27. However, the key issue here is that the dormer extension will fall within the
setting3 of the listed building and, indeed, it would be in very close
proximity to it. For reasons outlined above, the dormer would be
incongruent and its introduction into the immediate setting of the listed
building means that there would be a negative effect on the setting, albeit
at the lesser end of the scale of harm.
28. This means that there is conflict with BIP policies HE1 (Protecting listed
buildings and places, and their settings) and SP4 (Protecting and promoting
island identity), as the development would fail to protect the listed
building’s setting, and none of the policy HE1 exceptions would be
applicable in this case. I do appreciate that the appellant may be frustrated
by the seemingly protracted state of disuse of the listed building and the
need for repairs, but that does not provide a basis for not applying, or
diluting, the application of policies HE1 and SP4.
3 Setting being that defined in the BIP Glossary (page 359)
5

29. The planning authority’s second reason makes reference to overlooking and
loss of privacy effects from the dormer. However, given that the proposal is
intended to serve a bathroom internally, it would be reasonable to impose
planning conditions requiring obscure glazing and restricting opening lights,
to prevent overlooking of neighbours. As this matter could be addressed by
planning conditions, I am satisfied that the requirements of policy GD1
(Managing the health and wellbeing impact of new development), could be
addressed.
30. In conclusion on this element of the proposal, I agree with the planning
authority’s reason 1 and find that the dormer extension is unacceptable in
design terms and would result in a clear conflict with BIP policy GD6. I also
find that the dormer extension would fail to protect the setting of the
adjacent listed building and that it would conflict with BIP policies HE1 and
SP4.
31. It is worth noting here that my conclusion on this matter should not be
interpreted as a finding that a successful dormer roof extension is not
possible at this property, simply that the specific proposal is unacceptable in
design terms. In this regard, I note the Historic Environment Team’s
consultation response, which suggested that a smaller more traditionally
proportioned dormer could be considered.
North elevation – 2 new windows
32. The proposed 2 windows in the north elevation appear to relate to original
openings in the building (they are quite apparent internally). They would sit
directly above the private garden space of the ground floor unit and, if clear
glazed and openable, would result in unreasonable intrusion and loss of
privacy.
33. However, the purpose of the windows appears to be solely to allow more
daylight into the open plan living space, which is currently limited as the 2
existing windows are relatively small and set within deep reveals. As the
windows are not needed to provide views or ventilation, a planning
condition requiring the windows to be fixed units with obscure glazing would
address any overlooking/privacy concerns, whilst achieving the objective of
improving internal lighting levels.
34. I have noted the views of the owner of Blanc Pignon to the north, including
possible tenant sensitivities. However, the proposed windows are set at a
much lower level and with a comfortable separation distance. In any event,
the scope for a planning condition, as outlined above, would fully address
any privacy concerns.
35. I conclude that, subject to an appropriate planning condition requiring the 2
windows to be fixed and obscure glazed, there would be no conflict with
policy GD1.
West elevation – Juliet window
36. The alteration of an existing window on the west elevation to create a Juliet
window raises no undue overlooking issues, as it faces the appellant’s
6

outbuilding. It is also of a neat and appropriate design. It is common ground
between the parties that this element is acceptable. I agree.
Conclusion and recommendation
37. For the reasons stated above, I assess that the proposed dormer roof
extension is unacceptable in design terms and its impact on the setting of
the adjacent listed building, meaning that it conflicts with policies GD6, HE1
and SP4. Whilst I am satisfied that overlooking/privacy impacts from the 2
proposed windows in the north elevation, and the dormer extension, could
be addressed by planning conditions, this would not render the proposal, as
submitted, acceptable due to the principal planning objections arising from
the proposed dormer extension.
Accordingly, I recommend that the appeal be DISMISSED and that the
refusal reasons be reduced and revised to read:
Reason 1: The proposed dormer extension, due to its scale, massing, and
visual dominance, would have a detrimental impact on the character of the
host building and context of the surrounding area, contrary to Policy GD6 of
the Bridging Island Plan (2022).
Reason 2: The proposed dormer extension would constitute a discordant
feature within the immediate setting of the adjacent heritage asset, Palm
Grove. This would be harmful to, and would fail to protect, that setting, in
conflict with Policies SP4 and HE1 of the Bridging Island Plan (2022).
P. Staddon
Mr Philip Staddon BSc, Dip, MBA, MRTPI
7
location: The Barn, Palm Grove, St. Helier
